events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Yuen. She is of East Asian (Cantonese) descent, born in Hong Kong, immigrated to the United States at age 32. She is a former smoker with a 12 pack-year history, having quit 18 years prior to diagnosis. She works as a high school math teacher. She has a family history of lung cancer in a paternal uncle. Her Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status is 1 at time of diagnosis.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma histology (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3), primary site: upper lobe of right lung (ICD-O-3 site code C34.1). TNM staging: T3 (tumor 5.8 cm with invasion of the parietal pleura), N3 (ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node involvement and contralateral mediastinal involvement on PET-CT), M0 (no distant metastases at diagnosis). Summary stage: Regional. Relevant biomarkers: EGFR L858R mutation detected in exon 21 (allele frequency 38.2%); PD-L1 TPS 5% (Dako 22C3); ALK by IHC negative; ROS1 by FISH negative. Confirmation: Positive histology via CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of primary mass. Tumor size: 58 mm. Laterality: Right. Tumor location: Right upper lobe, abutting chest wall. Pleural effusion: None. Histologic grade: Moderately differentiated (Grade 2). Lymphovascular invasion: Present.

At age 63 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided core needle biopsy of right upper lobe mass (3 cores submitted). Microscopic description: Cores show infiltrating adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns. Tumor cells are columnar with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and moderate pale cytoplasm. No squamous or small cell differentiation identified. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive (diffuse, strong), Napsin-A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative, Synaptophysin negative, Chromogranin negative. Mucin stain: focally positive. EGFR by PCR (cobas): EGFR L858R mutation detected (exon 21). ALK IHC (D5F3): Negative. ROS1 IHC: Negative. PD-L1 22C3: TPS 5%. Final diagnosis: Invasive adenocarcinoma, right upper lobe, with EGFR L858R mutation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body with diagnostic CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) performed for initial staging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass measuring 5.8 x 4.2 cm, hypermetabolic (SUVmax 14.3). Right hilar lymph nodes enlarged, hypermetabolic (SUVmax 7.8, up to 1.9 cm short axis). Right paratracheal lymph nodes (4R) hypermetabolic (SUVmax 6.1). Left paratracheal (4L) node mildly hypermetabolic (SUVmax 3.4, borderline). Right supraclavicular lymph node hypermetabolic (SUVmax 5.2, 1.3 cm). No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No osseous lesions. No pleural effusion. Impression: Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer with ipsilateral supraclavicular (N3) nodal involvement. No evidence of distant (M1) disease.

At age 63 years, the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast performed at initial staging workup. Findings: No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass effect or midline shift. Ventricles and sulci are normal in caliber for age. Small scattered T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the periventricular white matter consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Impression: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) performed for non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, based on a specimen obtained from the primary right upper lobe mass via CT-guided core needle biopsy. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4 mutations/Mb (Low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, pathogenic, allele frequency 38.2%). Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (exon 7, pathogenic, allele frequency 42.1%); SMARCA4 p.R1192H (exon 25, variant of uncertain significance); CDH1 p.E243K (variant of uncertain significance). Copy number alterations: MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4, low-level, not meeting threshold for high amplification); CCND1 gain. No EGFR exon 19 deletion. No EGFR T790M detected. No KRAS mutation (consistent with EGFR-mutant status). No ALK, ROS1, RET, MET exon 14 skip, BRAF V600E, NTRK1/2/3 fusions identified. No ERBB2 amplification or mutation. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD): Not detected. Relevant negative findings: No STK11, KEAP1, or NF1 mutations detected.

At age 63 years, the patient had a new patient oncology consultation. History and physical: Patient presents with a 3-month history of progressive cough, mild hemoptysis (two episodes), and 8-pound unintentional weight loss. She has exertional dyspnea but no chest pain. Performance status ECOG 1. On exam: Decreased breath sounds right apex, no pleural rub. Palpable right supraclavicular lymph node approximately 1 cm, firm, non-tender. No hepatomegaly. Discussion: Staging workup complete. Diagnosis of Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R mutant. Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed case. Given N3 (supraclavicular) nodal disease, patient is not a surgical candidate. Case discussed with radiation oncology. Given presence of targetable EGFR mutation (L858R), tumor board recommendation is to proceed with osimertinib monotherapy per LAURA trial paradigm and available evidence supporting EGFR TKI in EGFR-mutant stage III unresectable NSCLC. Risks and benefits discussed at length. Patient is agreeable. Cancer is present at this assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient initiated curative-intent (definitive) osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line therapy for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma. Treatment rationale: EGFR-activating mutation (L858R), Stage IIIB unresectable disease. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed but patient declined given toxicity concerns. Osimertinib chosen for its CNS penetration and superior efficacy over first-generation EGFR TKIs.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks after osimertinib initiation), the patient developed grade 1-2 acneiform rash (paronychia right index finger, grade 1; facial papulopustular rash, grade 1) and grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day above baseline). Managed with topical clindamycin 1% solution to face, urea-based moisturizer, and dietary modifications. No dose reduction required. Rash improved to grade 1 by week 6. These are expected class-effect toxicities of EGFR TKI therapy.

At age 63 years (3 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass has decreased from 5.8 x 4.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.4 cm (RECIST 1.1: 47% decrease in longest diameter). Right hilar lymph nodes decreased in size (previously up to 1.9 cm, now 0.9 cm). Right paratracheal node (4R) decreased (now 0.7 cm, previously 1.4 cm). Right supraclavicular node no longer measurable on CT. No new lesions. No pleural effusion. No hepatic or adrenal lesions. Impression: Partial response to osimertinib per RECIST 1.1. Significant radiographic response in primary tumor and regional lymph nodes.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reports improved energy and resolution of hemoptysis. Cough is significantly improved. Weight has stabilized; she has regained 4 of the 8 pounds lost. Performance status ECOG 0-1. Review of imaging confirms partial response. No significant toxicity. Plan: Continue osimertinib 80 mg daily. Repeat imaging in 3 months. Cancer is present at this assessment but responding to therapy.

At age 63 years (6 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass has further decreased to 2.0 x 1.6 cm (RECIST 1.1: 65% decrease from baseline longest diameter). Right hilar lymph nodes remain small (0.6 cm). Previously enlarged mediastinal and supraclavicular nodes no longer pathologically enlarged. No new lesions. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous lesions. Impression: Continued partial response to osimertinib, approaching near-complete response in lymph nodes. Primary tumor residual mass may represent treatment-related fibrosis versus residual viable tumor.

At age 64 years (12 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for annual restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe residual mass stable at 1.8 x 1.5 cm (minimal change from 6-month scan). No interval new lesions in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Right hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes remain within normal limits. No hepatic lesions. No new adrenal lesions. Impression: Stable partial response. No evidence of progressive disease. MRI brain also obtained: No intracranial lesions identified. Ventricles and sulci normal.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an urgent MRI brain with and without gadolinium for new neurological symptoms (headaches, mild word-finding difficulties). Findings: Three new enhancing lesions identified: (1) left frontal lobe, 12 mm, with surrounding vasogenic edema; (2) right cerebellar hemisphere, 8 mm, no significant edema; (3) left parietal lobe, 6 mm. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Mild mass effect from left frontal lesion without midline shift. Impression: New intracranial metastases (3 lesions), consistent with metastatic non-small cell lung carcinoma. Concurrent CT chest also obtained: Right upper lobe mass has increased from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm (longest diameter), new right paratracheal lymph node 1.1 cm. Findings consistent with systemic progressive disease.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation, at time of progression), the patient underwent liquid biopsy (cell-free DNA, Guardant360 platform). Result: EGFR T790M resistance mutation detected (allele frequency 0.6%). EGFR L858R detected (allele frequency 1.2%). TP53 R248W detected (allele frequency 1.1%). No MET amplification detected (below threshold). No EGFR C797S detected. No PIK3CA mutation. No BRAF mutation. No RET fusion. Interpretation: Acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation detected, consistent with acquired resistance to first/second-line EGFR TKI therapy. Note: Patient was on osimertinib (third-generation TKI), and T790M detection in this context is unusual. This may represent residual T790M clone (pre-existing) or a distinct resistance mechanism. Molecular tumor board consultation requested.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an urgent oncology visit for disease progression. She reports 3 weeks of worsening headaches, predominantly bifrontal, worse in the morning, partially responsive to acetaminophen. She also notes mild word-finding difficulty over the past 2 weeks but no focal weakness, vision changes, or seizures. Performance status ECOG 1. Exam: Oriented x3, mild anomic aphasia noted on testing, no focal motor deficits, no papilledema. CT chest confirms systemic progression. MRI brain confirms 3 new brain metastases. Liquid biopsy reveals acquired T790M (unusual with osimertinib; likely reflecting heterogeneous resistance mechanism). Osimertinib discontinued. Patient started on dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for cerebral edema. Referral placed to radiation oncology for stereotactic radiosurgery evaluation. Multidisciplinary tumor board case reviewed. Plan: SRS to brain metastases followed by reassessment of systemic therapy. Given unusual T790M acquisition on osimertinib, likely multiple resistance mechanisms. Plan for tissue re-biopsy of primary lesion if feasible after SRS. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years (approximately 6 weeks after progression diagnosis), the patient underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) using Gamma Knife Icon system for three intracranial metastases. Treatment details: (1) Left frontal lobe lesion (12 mm): 20 Gy in 1 fraction, prescription isodose 50%; (2) Right cerebellar hemisphere lesion (8 mm): 20 Gy in 1 fraction; (3) Left parietal lobe lesion (6 mm): 22 Gy in 1 fraction. Immobilization: Thermoplastic mask. Imaging guidance: MRI-based planning with gadolinium. No intraoperative complications. Patient tolerated procedure well. Post-SRS dexamethasone taper planned over 2 weeks.

At age 64 years (2 weeks post-SRS), the patient developed grade 1 radiation-related fatigue and transient worsening of headache in the immediate post-SRS period, consistent with expected post-treatment inflammatory response. Managed with continuation of dexamethasone taper and analgesics. Word-finding difficulties improved with steroid therapy. No seizures, no focal neurological deficits, no radiation necrosis requiring intervention at this time. She completed dexamethasone taper without neurological deterioration.

At age 64 years (8 weeks after progression, following SRS), the patient underwent CT-guided core needle re-biopsy of the enlarged right upper lobe mass and enlarged right paratracheal lymph node. Pathology result: Right upper lobe mass: Invasive adenocarcinoma, morphologically similar to original biopsy. IHC: TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive. Molecular testing (rapid PCR): EGFR L858R confirmed; EGFR T790M not detected by PCR (below detection threshold of 1%). Note: T790M may be present at low level as suggested by liquid biopsy. Full NGS re-profiling sent. Paratracheal lymph node: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with lung primary.

At age 64 years (re-biopsy at progression), the patient had next generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) performed on re-biopsy tissue from right upper lobe mass. Tumor mutational burden: 6 mutations/Mb (Low-intermediate). Microsatellite status: MSS. Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, pathogenic, allele frequency 31.4%). Resistance mutations: EGFR T790M detected at low allele frequency (3.1%, below standard threshold for high-level resistance but above background). EGFR C797S: Not detected. New alterations vs. baseline: PIK3CA p.E545K (exon 9, pathogenic, allele frequency 18.2%) -- newly acquired, not present on initial NGS; PTEN p.R130Q (exon 5, pathogenic) -- newly acquired. Previously identified co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (persistent, allele frequency 35.1%); SMARCA4 p.R1192H (persistent). Copy number changes: MET amplification now high-level (copy number ratio 5.1, FISH confirmatory testing sent); CCND1 gain (persistent). No new fusions detected (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3 all negative). Interpretation: Mixed/heterogeneous resistance to osimertinib with low-level T790M, newly acquired PIK3CA/PTEN pathway activation, and high-level MET amplification. This is consistent with polyclonal acquired resistance and represents a therapeutically challenging scenario.

At age 64 years (post-SRS, re-biopsy results available), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment planning. She reports improved headaches since SRS and dexamethasone taper completion. Performance status ECOG 1. She remains motivated and is interested in clinical trial participation. Repeat NGS reveals complex heterogeneous resistance to osimertinib: low-level T790M, MET amplification (high-level), and newly acquired PIK3CA/PTEN alterations. Discussed at molecular tumor board. Given MET amplification as likely dominant resistance driver, options discussed: (1) Platinum-based doublet chemotherapy as bridging therapy; (2) MET inhibitor combination; (3) Enrollment in clinical trial targeting MET-amplified NSCLC post-EGFR TKI progression. Decision: Initiate carboplatin/pemetrexed as bridging systemic therapy. Active enrollment in a Phase II trial of savolitinib plus osimertinib for MET-amplified EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-progression being pursued. Cancer present, progressing systemically but CNS disease under local treatment.

At age 64 years (approximately 10 weeks after progression diagnosis), the patient initiated palliative-intent carboplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2 IV day 1) every 21 days as second-line systemic therapy for Stage IV (M1b brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, following acquired resistance to osimertinib. Vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks and folic acid 400 mcg orally daily initiated as per pemetrexed protocol. Dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily on day -1, day 0, and day +1 of each cycle as pemetrexed antifolate prophylaxis.

At age 64 years (cycle 1-2 of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient developed grade 2 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1.1 x 10^9/L on day 14 of cycle 1) without fever or infection. Grade 1 fatigue. Grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron 8 mg orally three times daily as needed. No grade 3 or 4 hematologic toxicities. No renal toxicity (creatinine stable). Cycle 2 administered on schedule without dose reduction. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) prophylaxis (filgrastim) added starting cycle 2 given grade 2 neutropenia in cycle 1.

At age 64 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed, approximately 9 weeks after chemotherapy initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain for restaging. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass has decreased from 2.7 cm to 2.1 cm. Right paratracheal node decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm. No new thoracic lesions. No hepatic or adrenal lesions. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion decreased from 12 mm to 7 mm, decreased surrounding edema. Right cerebellar lesion stable at 6 mm (decreased from 8 mm). Left parietal lesion now 3 mm (decreased from 6 mm). No new intracranial lesions. Impression: Partial response in chest (RECIST: 22% decrease in primary tumor). Continued response/stable disease in brain metastases post-SRS. No evidence of leptomeningeal disease.

At age 64 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reports tolerating chemotherapy reasonably well with manageable fatigue. Appetite is decreased. Weight loss of 3 pounds since starting chemotherapy. She is eager to transition to a clinical trial. Performance status ECOG 1. Imaging shows partial response in chest and stable/responding CNS disease. Clinical trial site contacted: She has been pre-screened for a Phase II investigator-sponsored trial of savolitinib (MET inhibitor) in combination with osimertinib rechallenge for MET-amplified, EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-third-generation TKI progression. She meets eligibility criteria pending final screening labs. Plan: Complete one additional cycle of carboplatin/pemetrexed (cycle 4), then transition to clinical trial. Cancer present, responding to current therapy.

At age 64 years (approximately 5 months after progression from osimertinib), the patient was enrolled and initiated treatment on a Phase II single-arm clinical trial: Savolitinib 300 mg orally once daily plus osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily for MET-amplified EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma following progression on third-generation EGFR TKI. Carboplatin/pemetrexed discontinued after 4 cycles given transition to trial. Trial ID: NCT (investigator-sponsored, institutional protocol). Rationale: High-level MET amplification as dominant resistance mechanism identified on repeat NGS; combination addresses both MET bypass and residual EGFR-dependent signaling. Consent obtained; patient enrolled after 4 cycles of bridging chemotherapy.

At age 64 years (4 weeks after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral edema (bilateral lower extremity, pitting, to mid-shin) consistent with savolitinib-related toxicity. Grade 1 nausea. Grade 1 fatigue. Acneiform rash returned to grade 1 consistent with osimertinib rechallenge. Peripheral edema managed with dietary sodium restriction and compression stockings; furosemide 20 mg orally daily was initiated. No dose interruption required. Edema improved to grade 1 over the following 3 weeks. Echocardiogram obtained: Normal LV function (EF 60-65%), no pericardial effusion.

At age 64 years (8 weeks after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain for protocol-specified on-trial restaging. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.4 cm (33% decrease from start of trial; 76% decrease from original baseline at diagnosis). Right paratracheal lymph node no longer measurable. No new lesions. No hepatic or adrenal disease. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion further decreased to 4 mm, no surrounding edema. Right cerebellar lesion 4 mm (stable). Left parietal lesion no longer visualized. No new intracranial lesions. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Impression: Partial response per RECIST 1.1 on trial. Favorable CNS response. Assessment: Best overall response to trial therapy is partial response. Patient continues trial therapy.

At age 64 years (8-week on-trial restaging), the patient had an on-trial oncology visit. She reports improved energy and reduced edema with furosemide. Weight is stable. She is tolerating the combination reasonably well. Performance status ECOG 1. Imaging confirms partial response; all lesions have decreased or resolved. Notably, the dominant MET-amplified resistant clone appears to be responding to savolitinib. She remains on full doses of both agents. Plan: Continue savolitinib plus osimertinib per protocol. Next restaging in 8 weeks. She is encouraged by the radiographic response. Cancer present, responding to clinical trial therapy. Molecular correlative samples (ctDNA, PBMC) collected per protocol.

At age 65 years (6 months after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient had protocol-specified restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain. CT chest: Right upper lobe residual mass stable at 1.3 cm. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. No new lesions in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion now 2 mm (essentially resolved). Right cerebellar lesion stable at 4 mm. No new intracranial lesions. No leptomeningeal disease. Impression: Continued partial response/stable disease. No evidence of progressive disease at 6-month on-trial assessment. Patient is doing well clinically. Overall: Best RECIST response is partial response, maintained for 6+ months on trial.

At age 65 years (6-month on-trial assessment), the patient had a protocol visit and oncology assessment. She is tolerating savolitinib plus osimertinib well. Peripheral edema remains controlled on furosemide. Grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 rash are present but acceptable to patient. Performance status ECOG 1. She has returned to teaching part-time and reports acceptable quality of life. Imaging confirms ongoing partial response at 6 months on trial. ctDNA from most recent blood draw: EGFR L858R allele frequency declining (0.3%), MET amplification below detection threshold -- encouraging molecular response. Plan: Continue trial therapy. Next restaging in 8 weeks. Cancer present (residual disease), responding to therapy. Patient in ongoing partial response on Phase II clinical trial.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Yuen
DOB: [redacted] | Age: 63
Diagnosis: Stage IIIB Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th ed.)
Treating Oncologist: [attending name on file]
Visit type: Established patient, oncology follow-up (3-month restaging)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old woman with known Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant right lung adenocarcinoma, presenting today for her 3-month restaging visit while on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Yuen was initially diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) right upper lobe adenocarcinoma. At that time she presented with a 3-month history of progressive cough, two episodes of mild hemoptysis, and approximately 8 pounds of unintentional weight loss with exertional dyspnea. Staging workup with whole-body PET-CT confirmed a 5.8 x 4.2 cm hypermetabolic right upper lobe mass with right hilar, right paratracheal, and right supraclavicular nodal involvement (N3 disease). Brain MRI was negative for intracranial metastases at the time of diagnosis. CT-guided biopsy confirmed invasive adenocarcinoma with EGFR L858R mutation on exon 21 (cobas PCR, allele frequency 38.2%). PD-L1 TPS was 5% by 22C3. Comprehensive genomic profiling via FoundationOne CDx additionally identified a co-occurring TP53 R248W mutation and low-level MET copy gain (ratio 2.4, not meeting threshold for amplification).

Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed the case and given her N3 (supraclavicular) nodal involvement, she was deemed a non-surgical candidate. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed at length; however, the patient declined given concerns about toxicity. Given the presence of a sensitizing EGFR mutation (L858R), the decision was made to initiate osi 80 mg orally once daily as definitive first-line systemic therapy, consistent with evolving evidence supporting EGFR TKI use in unresectable stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

She started Tagrisso approximately 3 months ago. Approximately 3 weeks after initiation she developed expected class-effect toxicities: a grade 1 acneiform papulopustular rash across the face (managed with topical clindamycin 1% solution and urea-based moisturizer), grade 1 paronychia of the right index finger, and grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline, managed with dietary modification). No dose reduction was required. She reports that the facial rash has now improved substantially -- she describes it as much less noticeable compared to weeks 3-4 and notes it has not interfered significantly with her daily activities.

Regarding her cancer-related symptoms: the cough that was her primary presenting complaint has improved markedly, and she reports it is now only intermittent and mild, occurring maybe once or twice per day. There have been no further episodes of hemoptysis since starting treatment. Her energy level has improved substantially -- she describes herself as feeling "more like herself" and has been able to take daily walks of 20-30 minutes. She reports that her appetite has improved and she has regained approximately 4 of the 8 pounds she had lost. She denies chest pain, new shortness of breath at rest, or worsening exertional dyspnea compared to her initial presentation.

She denies any new neurological symptoms: no headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness, no dizziness. She continues to work part-time in a tutoring capacity from home while on leave from her full-time teaching position.

She completed restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast prior to today's visit. Results reviewed below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports improved energy; weight has stabilized and trending upward (+4 lbs recovered). No night sweats. Low-grade fatigue present but improved compared to baseline at diagnosis.
Pulmonary: Cough markedly improved, now intermittent and mild. No hemoptysis. Mild exertional dyspnea present but improved -- able to walk on level ground without significant limitation. No pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no lower extremity edema, no chest pressure.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent loose stools (1-2 times daily, now grade 1 or less), improved from early treatment weeks. No blood in stool, no nausea or vomiting. Appetite improving.
Skin/Integument: Facial papulopustular rash improving; now mild (grade 1 or less). Paronychia of right index finger remains present but not worsening, patient using warm water soaks and topical antibiotic. No other skin toxicities.
Neurological: No headaches, no cognitive changes, no focal neurological symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
Other systems: No dysuria, no new lymphadenopathy noted by patient, no oral mucositis.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

1. Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily -- EGFR TKI, started 3 months ago
2. Topical clindamycin phosphate 1% solution -- applied to face twice daily for acneiform rash
3. Urea 20% cream -- applied to affected skin areas once daily
4. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for minor discomfort (paronychia)
5. Multivitamin with vitamin D -- daily

Allergies: NKDA (no known drug allergies)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.7 degrees C, SpO2 97% on room air, height 160 cm, weight 56.2 kg (up from 54.0 kg at last visit approximately 6 weeks ago).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing East Asian woman in no acute distress. Affect is pleasant and engaged. She is dressed neatly and appears well.

HEENT: Scant residual papulopustular lesions across bilateral cheeks and forehead, consistent with healing grade 1 acneiform rash -- much improved from prior examination. No oral mucosal ulcerations. Pupils equal and reactive to light.

Neck/Lymphatics: Previously palpable right supraclavicular lymph node -- today this is no longer clearly palpable. No cervical or left supraclavicular adenopathy. No axillary adenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No S3 or S4. No jugular venous distension.

Pulmonary: Breath sounds are present bilaterally and improved -- previously noted decreased air entry at the right apex. Today there is still mildly diminished breath sounds over the right upper lobe region, but improved compared to initial examination. No wheezes, crackles, or pleural rub. Good air movement overall.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds active.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis. Right index finger with mild periungual erythema (grade 1 paronychia), stable -- no fluctuance, no purulence.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Gait is normal. No ataxia. Speech is fluent and articulate. No cerebellar signs on finger-nose-finger testing.

Skin: As above -- residual grade 1 rash on face, improving.

---

LABORATORY DATA (drawn within 1 week of today's visit)

Complete Blood Count:
  WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L (normal)
  Hemoglobin: 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, stable compared to prior)
  Platelets: 248 x10^9/L (normal)
  ANC: 4.2 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  Creatinine: 0.74 mg/dL (eGFR >60, stable)
  BUN: 14 mg/dL
  Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
  AST: 22 U/L (normal)
  Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
  Albumin: 3.7 g/dL
  Glucose: 94 mg/dL

LFTs: Within normal limits. No hepatotoxicity attributable to osi.
Electrolytes: Normal; specifically QTc-related electrolytes (K, Mg) within normal limits.

Magnesium: 2.1 mg/dL (normal).

ECG: QTc 428 ms (normal, no interval prolongation of concern).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (reviewed today, obtained prior to this visit):

Compared to pre-treatment baseline (initial staging PET-CT):

Chest: The right upper lobe mass has decreased substantially in size, now measuring 3.1 x 2.4 cm in its longest cross-sectional dimensions, down from 5.8 x 4.2 cm at diagnosis -- roughly a 47% reduction in the longest dimension. The mass appears less solid and more heterogeneous centrally, which may reflect areas of treatment-related necrosis or fibrosis. There is no significant pleural effusion. No endobronchial lesion identified.

Hilar and Mediastinal Nodes: Right hilar lymph nodes have decreased significantly and now measure approximately 0.9 cm in short axis (previously up to 1.9 cm). Right paratracheal (4R) station node is now 0.7 cm in short axis (previously approximately 1.4 cm on PET-CT). The previously hypermetabolic right supraclavicular node is no longer clearly measurable on this CT (consistent with the finding today that the node is no longer palpable on exam).

Abdomen and Pelvis: No hepatic lesions identified. No adrenal lesions. No abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. No ascites.

Bones (partially imaged): No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions within the field of view.

Overall Impression: Excellent radiographic response to osi -- the primary right upper lobe tumor has shrunk by nearly half and the regional lymph nodes have decreased markedly across all previously involved stations. No new sites of disease have appeared in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. This is consistent with a meaningful partial response to treatment.

(Note: Brain MRI not obtained at this 3-month interval as per plan; will reassess CNS surveillance timing at 6-12 months per current evidence.)

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant (T3 N3 M0) -- Currently on Tagrisso 80 mg daily, cycle 3 (month 3)

Ms. Yuen has had an excellent early response to osi. Imaging today confirms a substantial partial response -- the right upper lobe primary has shrunk from 5.8 cm to 3.1 cm (longest dimension), and previously enlarged hilar, mediastinal, and supraclavicular nodes are now either below the threshold for pathologic enlargement or not visible at all. The clinical response correlates nicely with her symptom improvement: cough resolved, hemoptysis resolved, weight recovery in progress, and performance status improved to ECOG 0-1.

I reviewed these results with Ms. Yuen in detail today. She expressed great relief and was visibly encouraged by the findings. We discussed the significance of the tumor response and that Tagrisso works by blocking the EGFR pathway that is driving her cancer's growth.

Plan:
- Continue osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily without dose modification. No toxicity necessitating adjustment at this time.

2. Acneiform rash (grade 1, improving) -- class-effect EGFR TKI toxicity
- Continue topical clindamycin 1% and urea-based moisturizer.
- Encouraged sun protection (SPF 30+, wide-brimmed hat) given photosensitivity risk with ongoing EGFR inhibition.
- Patient educated to contact clinic promptly if rash worsens to grade 2 or above (covering >30% BSA, significantly painful, requiring systemic antibiotics).

3. Grade 1 paronychia, right index finger
- Continue warm water soaks twice daily and topical antibiotic (mupirocin 2% ointment added today as topical clindamycin is being used for facial rash; using same product for both is reasonable but I prefer mupirocin for focal periungual infection).

- Referral to dermatology if worsening or development of additional paronychia affecting multiple digits.

4. Grade 1 diarrhea (ongoing but improved)
- Continue dietary modifications (low-fiber diet, BRAT-adjacent diet adjustments).
- Patient has loperamide on hand for use as needed; instructed to use if stools increase to 4+ above baseline per day.
- Adequate hydration discussed.

5. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL)
- Mild, likely multifactorial (chronic disease, prior nutritional depletion from weight loss). Stable on serial labs.
- No transfusion threshold met. Monitor at next visit. Iron studies may be considered if progressive.

6. Cancer status
- Cancer is present at this assessment but responding well to targeted therapy.
- Prognosis discussed -- EGFR-mutant NSCLC on Tagrisso has demonstrated median progression-free survival data in the range of 18-24 months in advanced-stage trials; in stage III disease (LAURA trial data and related evidence), duration of benefit may be prolonged. Patient understands this is not curative but that meaningful disease control is a realistic goal.
- Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and is in good spirits. She returns to the clinic in 3 months or sooner if new symptoms arise.

Follow-up: Approximately 3 months for repeat imaging and clinical assessment.
Emergency contact: On file. Patient given oncology on-call number for urgent issues.

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
--------------------
Specimen ID:        MP-LBX-[ACCESSION]
Specimen Type:      Plasma-derived cell-free DNA (cfDNA); peripheral blood
Collection Method:  Peripheral venous blood draw; two 10 mL Streck Cell-Free DNA BCT tubes collected
Volume Collected:   20 mL whole blood (two tubes)
Sample Quality:     Acceptable. cfDNA yield: 18.4 ng/mL plasma. Plasma volume processed: 4.2 mL.
Assay Platform:     Guardant360 (Guardant Health, Redwood City, CA) — next-generation sequencing-based liquid biopsy, 74-gene panel
Ordering Provider:  [Attending Medical Oncologist, Thoracic Oncology]
Clinical Indication: EGFR-mutant (L858R) Stage IV non-small cell lung carcinoma (adenocarcinoma), right upper lobe primary, on osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily x 14 months. New imaging evidence of intracranial metastases (3 lesions on MRI brain) and systemic progression (right upper lobe mass increased from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm; new right paratracheal lymphadenopathy). Liquid biopsy obtained to evaluate for acquired resistance mechanisms prior to modification of systemic therapy.

CLINICAL CONTEXT
----------------
Patient is a 64-year-old woman of East Asian (Cantonese) descent with a history of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, harboring EGFR L858R mutation (exon 21) and co-mutations TP53 p.R248W, SMARCA4 p.R1192H (per initial FoundationOne CDx NGS). ALK IHC negative, ROS1 IHC negative, PD-L1 TPS 5% at diagnosis. TMB 4 mut/Mb. MET low-level amplification on initial NGS (copy number ratio 2.4, below high-amplification threshold). Patient was initiated on osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line monotherapy for unresectable Stage IIIB EGFR-mutant NSCLC; achieved a partial response at 3 months (primary tumor 47% reduction) and maintained partial/stable response through 12 months. At 14 months, imaging demonstrates systemic and CNS progression as noted above. This cfDNA assay is submitted to characterize acquired resistance mechanisms.

REFERENCE METHOD / ASSAY DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------
Guardant360 is a validated Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA)-certified, College of American Pathologists (CAP)-accredited liquid biopsy assay. The assay analyzes single nucleotide variants (SNVs), indels, fusions, copy number amplifications, and microsatellite instability in 74 cancer-related genes from circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) isolated from plasma. Analytical sensitivity for point mutations is approximately 0.1% variant allele frequency (VAF) in samples with adequate ctDNA yield. Results are reported for alterations with VAF >= 0.15% or meeting platform-defined thresholds for copy number changes and fusions. Variants of uncertain significance are reported where applicable.

DETECTED ALTERATIONS
---------------------

1. EGFR p.L858R (c.2573T>G), Exon 21
   Classification:   Pathogenic — Sensitizing/Oncogenic Mutation (Driver)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF):   1.2%
   Interpretation:   This is the founding oncogenic driver mutation in this patient's NSCLC. Detection of L858R at VAF 1.2% in ctDNA confirms ongoing ctDNA shedding from EGFR-mutant tumor and is consistent with active disease at the time of progression. Reduction from the presumed tumor fraction at diagnosis is consistent with prior treatment effect; however, the increase in measurable disease on imaging correlates with detectable ctDNA elevation.

2. EGFR p.T790M (c.2369C>T), Exon 20
   Classification:   Pathogenic — Acquired Resistance Mutation
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF):   0.6%
   Interpretation:   EGFR T790M is a well-characterized acquired resistance mutation in patients treated with first- and second-generation EGFR TKIs (erlo, Iressa, afat). Its presence in a patient treated with osi (a third-generation, T790M-active TKI) is ATYPICAL and warrants careful interpretation. Possible explanations include: (a) a pre-existing low-level T790M sub-clone present at diagnosis that was not detected by tissue-based PCR (cobas threshold ~1%) or initial NGS (FoundationOne CDx, below detection), which may have been present at sub-clonal frequency and not appreciably suppressed by osi monotherapy; (b) T790M arising as a secondary event in a heterogeneous resistance context; or (c) a co-occurring resistance mechanism in which T790M represents one component of polyclonal acquired resistance. Given the low VAF of 0.6% and the clinical context, T790M alone is unlikely to be the dominant resistance driver. Correlation with tissue re-biopsy is strongly recommended. Molecular tumor board review recommended.
   Clinical Note:    T790M at this low VAF in the context of Tagrisso therapy should not be interpreted as a definitive indication for further T790M-targeting; osi achieves robust T790M suppression. Additional resistance mechanisms should be presumed.

3. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T), Exon 7
   Classification:   Pathogenic — Tumor Suppressor Loss-of-Function (Co-mutation; present at baseline)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF):   1.1%
   Interpretation:   This TP53 hotspot mutation was identified in the original FoundationOne CDx report (VAF 42.1% in tissue) and is concordantly detected here in ctDNA, confirming clonal persistence. The lower VAF in plasma compared to tissue reflects the dilutional effect of non-tumor cfDNA. TP53 p.R248W is a dominant negative gain-of-function mutation associated with more aggressive tumor behavior and is a known co-mutation in EGFR-mutant NSCLC that may contribute to therapeutic resistance.

-----------------------------------------
The following alterations were NOT detected above the assay's limit of detection:

- EGFR Exon 19 deletion:   Not detected
- EGFR p.C797S (Exon 20):  Not detected
  [Note: EGFR C797S is the most common mechanism of Tagrisso resistance in the T790M-positive setting; its absence may suggest an alternative resistance pathway or that T790M is not the dominant mechanism here.]
- EGFR Exon 20 insertion:  Not detected
- KRAS mutation (all codons):   Not detected [expected in EGFR-mutant NSCLC; consistent with known mutual exclusivity]
- ALK fusion:   Not detected
- ROS1 fusion:  Not detected
- RET fusion:   Not detected
- MET amplification:   Not detected above reporting threshold [note: low-level MET amplification was identified on original tissue NGS at copy number ratio 2.4; cfDNA copy number analysis has lower sensitivity than tissue-based methods for low-level amplifications; absence here does not exclude low-level MET amplification in tumor]
- BRAF p.V600E:  Not detected
- PIK3CA mutation:  Not detected

ADDITIONAL ASSAY METRICS
--------------------------
Tumor Mutational Burden (cfTMB):   Not reportable from this sample (cfTMB assessment requires adequate tumor fraction; current ctDNA fraction is at the lower range for cfTMB calculation; tissue-based TMB from prior FoundationOne CDx was 4 mutations/Mb [Low])
ctDNA Fraction (estimated):   Low (~1–2%, estimated from EGFR L858R VAF with correction for ploidy assumptions)
Assay QC:   PASS. All internal controls within acceptable ranges. Sufficient cfDNA input for variant calling.

SAMPLE ADEQUACY NOTE
----------------------
This sample contains detectable ctDNA (cfDNA yield 18.4 ng/mL; EGFR L858R detected at VAF 1.2%). While the overall ctDNA fraction is low, the assay's analytical sensitivity enables reliable detection of variants above 0.15% VAF. Low-level copy number changes (amplifications at low copy number ratios) and gene fusions may have reduced sensitivity at this ctDNA level. A negative result for any specific alteration does not exclude its presence at sub-threshold levels in the tumor.

INTERPRETATION AND SUMMARY
----------------------------
This liquid biopsy reveals a complex ctDNA profile at the time of confirmed radiographic progression on osi monotherapy. The key findings are:

1. Confirmed persistence of the founding EGFR L858R driver mutation (VAF 1.2%), consistent with active EGFR-mutant disease.
2. Detection of acquired EGFR T790M at low VAF (0.6%). This is unusual in the context of third-generation TKI (Tagrisso) therapy, which is T790M-active. The biological significance is uncertain; this may represent: (a) a pre-existing sub-clonal T790M not suppressed by osi at this low level; (b) a minor component of polyclonal resistance not serving as the dominant mechanism. The absence of EGFR C797S, which would be expected to co-occur with T790M as a tandem osi resistance mechanism, argues against T790M being the dominant driver.
3. TP53 p.R248W concordantly detected, consistent with baseline characterization.
4. No dominant classical osi resistance mechanism identified by this liquid biopsy alone (no C797S, no amplification detected, no bypass fusion). The molecular basis of progression remains incompletely defined from ctDNA at this ctDNA fraction.

CLINICAL CONSIDERATIONS
------------------------
Given the low ctDNA fraction and atypical/ambiguous resistance profile, tissue re-biopsy of the progressing lesion (right upper lobe mass and/or right paratracheal lymph node) with comprehensive repeat NGS is strongly recommended to:
  - Confirm or exclude high-level MET amplification (not detectable by cfDNA at this tumor fraction but suspected given low-level baseline amplification)
  - Identify additional bypass track mutations (PIK3CA, BRAF, RAS pathway) not detectable in plasma at this sensitivity
  - Characterize the full repertoire of acquired resistance mechanisms for treatment planning
  - Correlate tissue T790M frequency with plasma findings

Molecular tumor board consultation is recommended prior to next-line treatment selection.

DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY
-------------------
ACQUIRED EGFR T790M RESISTANCE MUTATION DETECTED at low allele frequency (VAF 0.6%) in plasma ctDNA. EGFR L858R (founding driver) and TP53 p.R248W (co-mutation) also detected. No dominant alternative resistance mechanism identified by liquid biopsy. Clinical correlation and tissue confirmation strongly recommended.

Given unresectable Stage III disease with a targetable EGFR mutation, the patient was started on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily approximately 14 months ago as definitive first-line therapy; concurrent chemoradiation was offered and discussed at length but declined by the patient due to concerns about toxicity. Over the past 2 weeks, she has noticed word-finding difficulty -- she describes struggling to retrieve names of students and common vocabulary words -- which has been distressing to her given her profession as a high school math teacher. She denies focal weakness, sensory changes, visual disturbance, ataxia, or seizure activity. She has had no syncope. She continued Tagrisso through this period. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

MS. MARGARET YUEN is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IV (previously III) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, presenting with imaging-confirmed systemic and intracranial disease progression following 14 months of osi (Tagrisso) monotherapy.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION ON Tagrisso -- NEW INTRACRANIAL METASTASES AND SYSTEMIC PROGRESSION

The patient has developed clear-cut progression on osi evidenced by:
- Three new brain metastases on gadolinium-enhanced MRI (left frontal 12 mm with edema, right cerebellar 8 mm, left parietal 6 mm) -- this represents new M1b disease, upstaging to Stage IV
- Growth of right upper lobe primary from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm
- New right paratracheal lymph node enlargement to 1.1 cm

The 14-month duration of response to osi monotherapy is broadly consistent with the median PFS seen in EGFR-mutant unresectable stage III/IV disease. The unusual finding of acquired EGFR T790M in the liquid biopsy in the context of osi resistance is noteworthy -- osi is a third-generation TKI specifically designed to inhibit T790M -- and likely reflects one of several possible scenarios: (a) a pre-existing minor subclonal T790M clone that was below detection at baseline and has expanded, (b) a newly acquired second-site T790M under different selective pressure than typical (unusual but documented), or (c) a heterogeneous resistance landscape in which T790M is co-present with other dominant resistance mechanisms. Additional tissue molecular profiling will be essential to characterize the full resistance landscape before determining the next systemic therapy approach.

IMPRESSION: Tagrisso resistance with multi-compartment progression. Staging revised to Stage IV (M1b, brain metastases).

PLAN:
- Tagrisso DISCONTINUED as of today.
- Dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily started immediately for management of vasogenic edema around left frontal lesion (and likely contributing to headaches and word-finding difficulty). Will monitor blood glucose given new steroid use; patient and husband educated on hyperglycemia symptoms and fingerstick monitoring.
- URGENT REFERRAL to Radiation Oncology for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) evaluation of all three intracranial metastases. Given the small number and sizes of lesions (3 lesions, largest 12 mm), SRS is the preferred approach over whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) to minimize neurocognitive toxicity in this relatively young, high-functioning patient. Neurosurgical opinion is not indicated given the pattern and size of lesions.
- Tissue re-biopsy of the right upper lobe mass is being planned to fully characterize the resistance molecular landscape at progression -- this is critical for next-line therapy selection. We will coordinate with interventional radiology after SRS is complete.
- Molecular tumor board case submission: The unusual T790M detection on Tagrisso with concurrent MET amplification low-level signal (from original NGS) and the emerging resistance picture warrants multidisciplinary genomic review. Case submitted for next molecular tumor board.

2. CEREBRAL EDEMA / NEUROLOGICAL SYMPTOMS

Patient's morning-predominant headaches and anomic aphasia are most consistent with vasogenic edema surrounding the left frontal metastasis. Neurological exam reassuringly intact beyond the word-finding difficulty -- no motor deficits, no papilledema, no evidence of herniation.

PLAN:
- Dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily as above. Standard approach; will taper after SRS.
- Acetaminophen continued as needed for headache.
- Patient instructed to return to emergency department or call immediately for: new focal weakness, vision changes, seizure, worsening confusion, or rapidly worsening headache.
- Antiseizure prophylaxis: not routinely indicated for brain metastases; will defer to radiation oncology and neuro-oncology input.

3. SYSTEMIC THERAPY PLANNING (post-SRS)

Next-line therapy will depend heavily on tissue molecular profiling of the resistant tumor. Key considerations:
- The low-level T790M in the liquid biopsy is ambiguous as a therapeutic target in the Tagrisso-relapsed setting (C797S is not detected, but standard Tagrisso re-challenge is not appropriate without further data)
- Low-level MET amplification was present on baseline NGS (copy number ratio 2.4); in the context of osi resistance, MET amplification frequently rises to high-level and becomes a dominant resistance driver -- the tissue biopsy will be critical to assess this
- PIK3CA pathway activation and EGFR-independent bypass mechanisms should be evaluated
- Standard options being considered include: platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (carbo/peme given adenocarcinoma histology), clinical trial enrollment targeting specific resistance mechanisms identified on repeat tissue NGS
- Immunotherapy with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade has limited evidence in EGFR-mutant NSCLC and PD-L1 5% is borderline low; less favored as monotherapy in this context
- Patient has expressed strong interest in clinical trial participation; will research currently open trials for EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-osi progression, particularly those targeting MET-amplified, EGFR-mutant disease if tissue re-biopsy confirms high-level MET amplification

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND MONITORING

- Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL): will monitor; likely anemia of chronic disease. Iron studies to be checked at next visit.
- Mild LDH elevation: non-specific but will trend.
- Blood glucose monitoring initiated given new dexamethasone.
- Nutrition: Patient has had some weight loss over the disease course. Referral to oncology dietitian placed.
- Social work: Referral placed given disease progression, work impact (word-finding difficulties affecting teaching), and emotional burden on patient and family.
- Patient and husband received extensive counseling today regarding the diagnosis of disease progression, the plan for SRS, and the path forward. Both are engaged and asking thoughtful questions. Patient is emotionally distressed but determined.

CANCER STATUS: Present, progressing. Staging revised from IIIB to Stage IV (M1b, intracranial metastases).

RETURN VISIT: Patient will be seen in radiation oncology consultation within the next 5-7 days. She will follow up in our clinic within 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms change. Tissue re-biopsy planning will be coordinated during this interval.

INTERPRETATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This re-biopsy specimen from a patient with EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma who progressed on osimertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI) reveals a pattern of complex, polyclonal acquired resistance. The genomic findings can be organized into three mechanistic categories:

1. BYPASS SIGNALING VIA MET AMPLIFICATION (Dominant Mechanism):
   High-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.1), markedly increased from low-level amplification at diagnosis (CNR 2.4), represents the most clinically significant resistance mechanism. MET amplification constitutes a bypass track that activates downstream RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling independently of EGFR suppression. The degree of amplification (CNR 5.1) crosses the threshold associated with MET inhibitor sensitivity in published clinical data (GEOMETRY mono-1, VISION, and investigator-sponsored MET-amplified NSCLC cohorts). This is consistent with clonal expansion of a pre-existing low-level MET-amplified subclone under osimertinib selection pressure. The co-occurrence of low-level T790M (AF 3.1%) in the context of high-level MET amplification and osimertinib treatment is likely a minor clonal finding and does not identify T790M as the dominant or actionable resistance driver in this case.

2. CONVERGENT PI3K-AKT-mTOR PATHWAY ACTIVATION (Secondary/Co-existing Mechanism):
   The concurrent acquisition of PIK3CA p.E545K (AF 18.2%, gain-of-function) and PTEN p.R130Q (AF 14.7%, loss-of-function) represents convergent biallelic PI3K pathway dysregulation. Both alterations are newly acquired and not present at diagnosis. Their co-emergence at similar allele frequencies suggests they may reside within the same or overlapping subclonal populations. PI3K pathway activation is a well-described EGFR TKI resistance mechanism that supports tumor cell survival downstream of EGFR and MET. The relative allele frequencies (18.2% and 14.7%) suggest that PI3K/AKT activation is operative in a substantial portion of the tumor.

3. LOW-LEVEL EGFR T790M (Minor Clone, Uncertain Clinical Significance):
   EGFR T790M is detected at AF 3.1% in tissue (above analytical noise, but well below the threshold conventionally used to define high-level T790M-mediated resistance, typically ≥10–20% tissue AF or concordant high-level ctDNA). The concurrent liquid biopsy detected T790M at 0.6% ctDNA AF. In the context of osimertinib (which itself targets T790M), high-level T790M would be paradoxical; the low-level detection here most likely represents either: (a) a minor pre-existing T790M clone that was suppressed but not fully eradicated by osimertinib, or (b) a newly emerging subpopulation whose contribution to the resistance phenotype is marginal relative to MET amplification and PI3K pathway activation. Absence of C797S confirms that tertiary on-target resistance via triple EGFR mutation (L858R/T790M/C797S) is not operative.

Overall Interpretation: This specimen demonstrates polyclonal acquired resistance to osimertinib driven predominantly by high-level MET amplification, with co-existing convergent PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway activation through PIK3CA gain-of-function and PTEN loss-of-function. The persistence of EGFR L858R and TP53 R248W confirms the adenocarcinomatous lineage and clonal continuity with the original tumor. TMB remains low (6 mut/Mb), arguing against an immunotherapy-sensitive phenotype based solely on TMB, though concurrent PD-L1 status from the re-biopsy specimen is not provided with this NGS submission and should be assessed separately. The complex resistance landscape has significant implications for therapeutic selection and is appropriate for multidisciplinary molecular tumor board review.

This test was performed using the FoundationOne CDx assay, a next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic device that sequences DNA from FFPE tumor tissue for the full coding sequences of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select gene rearrangements, genomic signatures (TMB, MSI, LOH), and copy number alterations across the genome. Analytical sensitivity: ≥5% allele frequency for substitutions (≥1% for select hotspots), ≥5% AF for insertions/deletions ≤40 bp. Copy number calling: amplifications defined at copy number ratio ≥2.5 or absolute copy number ≥6 relative to diploid state.

Note on low-AF variant (EGFR T790M at 3.1%): Detected above background noise but below standard clinical threshold. The detection of this variant is provided in the context of demonstrated liquid biopsy confirmation and clinical osimertinib progression; variant is judged to be tumor-derived based on allele frequency, read depth, and orthogonal ctDNA concordance. Clinical interpretation should account for the low allele frequency and probable minor clonal representation.

Specimen Adequacy: Adequate. Estimated tumor content 40% by pathology review. Sequencing coverage meets all quality thresholds. This report is clinically valid.

CLINICAL HISTORY:
Patient is a 63-year-old East Asian female, former smoker (12 pack-year history, quit 18 years prior), presenting with a 3-month history of progressive cough, two episodes of hemoptysis, and unintentional weight loss. CT chest demonstrated a right upper lobe mass measuring 5.8 x 4.2 cm with associated ipsilateral hilar, mediastinal, and right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. PET-CT confirms hypermetabolic right upper lobe mass (SUVmax 14.3) with N3 nodal involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on staging workup. Brain MRI negative for intracranial metastases. Procedure performed by interventional radiology under CT guidance. Clinical indication: tissue diagnosis of right upper lobe mass for definitive histologic characterization and molecular profiling.

A: Right upper lobe mass -- CT-guided core needle biopsy (3 cores in formalin)

GROSS DESCRIPTION:
Specimen A: Received in formalin, labeled with patient name and "right upper lobe mass, core biopsy." Three tan-white, firm cylindrical tissue cores measuring 0.9 cm, 1.1 cm, and 0.8 cm in length, each approximately 1.5 mm in diameter. All cores are entirely submitted in one cassette (A1). Representative sections are examined histologically with H&E staining. Additional unstained slides cut and retained for immunohistochemical and molecular studies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
Histologic sections from Specimen A reveal cores of pulmonary parenchyma containing an infiltrating epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is composed of atypical columnar to cuboidal cells arranged predominantly in irregular acinar/glandular formations (approximately 60% of tumor area) with a secondary lepidic growth component (approximately 25% of tumor area), characterized by neoplastic cells lining intact alveolar walls without stromal, pleural, or vascular invasion in the lepidic zones. A minor solid component is present (estimated 10-15%). Tumor cells display moderate nuclear pleomorphism with enlarged, irregular nuclei, vesicular chromatin, and prominent, often eccentrically placed nucleoli. The cytoplasm is moderate in amount and pale to finely granular. Mitotic figures are identified (2-3 per 10 high-power fields, with no atypical forms appreciated on limited material). Focal intracytoplasmic mucin is identified (Alcian blue/PAS stain: focally positive). Prominent lymphovascular invasion is identified in one of three cores, with clusters of malignant cells within endothelial-lined channels. No squamous differentiation, no intercellular bridges, no keratin pearl formation. No small cell or neuroendocrine morphology. Background lung parenchyma shows mild anthracotic pigment deposition and mild respiratory bronchiolitis, consistent with smoking history. No evidence of granulomatous inflammation. No organisms identified.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IV (M1b) EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, now presenting for oncology follow-up after completion of stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to three intracranial metastases and to review results of repeat tumor tissue biopsy with next-generation sequencing. The purpose of today's visit is multifold: to assess post-SRS neurological recovery, to review comprehensive re-profiling NGS data, and to finalize a second-line systemic therapy plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Yuen was originally diagnosed with Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) right upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR L858R point mutation (exon 21, allele frequency 38%) approximately 14 months ago. She initiated osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line therapy given her actionable EGFR mutation. She achieved a robust partial response, with her primary right upper lobe mass shrinking from 5.8 cm to approximately 1.8 cm and normalization of mediastinal and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. She had no evidence of systemic progression for 12 months on Tagrisso.

Approximately one month prior to her last visit, she developed new-onset bifrontal headaches (worse in the morning) and mild word-finding difficulties over two to three weeks. Urgent MRI brain at that time revealed three new enhancing intracranial lesions: a 12 mm left frontal lesion with surrounding vasogenic edema, an 8 mm right cerebellar lesion, and a 6 mm left parietal lesion. Concurrent CT chest demonstrated growth of the right upper lobe mass from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm and a new right paratracheal node measuring 1.1 cm, confirming concurrent systemic progression. Liquid biopsy (Guardant360) obtained at that time detected an EGFR T790M resistance mutation at a low allele frequency (0.6%), along with persistence of EGFR L858R (1.2%) and TP53 R248W (1.1%). osi was discontinued at that visit. She was started on dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for cerebral edema and was urgently referred to radiation oncology.

Approximately six weeks after progression was identified, she underwent Gamma Knife SRS to all three intracranial metastases: 20 Gy to the left frontal lesion, 20 Gy to the right cerebellar lesion, and 22 Gy to the left parietal lesion. She tolerated the procedure without acute complications. In the two weeks following SRS, she had grade 1 fatigue and transient worsening of headache, managed with a dexamethasone taper. She completed the steroid taper without neurological deterioration, and her word-finding difficulties have improved substantially.

Following SRS, she underwent CT-guided core needle re-biopsy of the right upper lobe mass (along with sampling of the right paratracheal lymph node) to characterize resistance mechanisms. Pathology from that re-biopsy confirmed invasive adenocarcinoma with TTF-1 positivity. Rapid PCR confirmed EGFR L858R; T790M was not detected at or above the PCR detection threshold (1%). Full tissue NGS (FoundationOne CDx) was sent from that re-biopsy specimen and results are now available today.

Today she presents specifically to review the NGS re-profiling results and to formulate a systemic therapy plan. She reports that headaches have largely resolved following SRS, and her word-finding is back to near-normal. She denies new headaches, visual changes, focal weakness, seizures, or ataxia. She has mild fatigue, which she attributes to the recent procedures and the emotional burden of disease progression. Appetite is somewhat decreased but stable. She has not lost significant weight since the last visit. She is not on any systemic cancer therapy at this time.

FoundationOne CDx re-profiling (tissue, right upper lobe mass):
- Tumor mutational burden: 6 mut/Mb (low-intermediate; increased from 4 mut/Mb at diagnosis)
- Microsatellite status: MSS
- EGFR L858R (exon 21): Confirmed, allele frequency 31.4%
- EGFR T790M: Detected at low allele frequency (3.1%) -- below standard high-level resistance threshold but above background; consistent with low-level sub-clonal T790M
- EGFR C797S: Not detected

- PTEN p.R130Q (exon 5, pathogenic): Newly acquired -- not present on initial NGS
- TP53 p.R248W: Persistent, allele frequency 35.1%
- SMARCA4 p.R1192H: Persistent (variant of uncertain significance)
- MET amplification: High-level (copy number ratio 5.1; FISH confirmatory testing pending at time of report)
- CCND1 gain: Persistent
- No new fusions detected (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3 all negative)
- No ERBB2 amplification or mutation

Interpretation: This NGS profile is consistent with polyclonal, heterogeneous acquired resistance to Tagrisso. The dominant likely resistance mechanism is high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.1), which is a well-established bypass signaling pathway enabling EGFR TKI resistance. Co-occurring newly acquired PIK3CA E545K and PTEN loss-of-function mutations suggest activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway as an additional resistance mechanism, which may also confer some degree of cross-resistance. Low-level T790M (3.1% AF on tissue; 0.6% on liquid biopsy) is present but at a level unlikely to be the dominant driver, especially in the context of Tagrisso therapy (which covers T790M). This represents a therapeutically complex scenario.

Case reviewed at molecular tumor board prior to today's visit. The consensus from the molecular tumor board is that high-level MET amplification is the dominant resistance driver and represents the primary therapeutic target. The PIK3CA/PTEN axis is a secondary concern and currently no approved targeted agent is available that specifically addresses this in combination with EGFR inhibition for this context.

ASSESSMENT
1. Stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma (right upper lobe primary, M1b with intracranial metastases), with acquired resistance to first-line osi at 14 months. Disease progression confirmed systemically (right upper lobe mass and mediastinal nodal recurrence) and intracranially (three brain metastases -- all treated with SRS approximately 6 weeks ago).

2. CNS disease: Post-SRS, patient is clinically improved. Headaches resolved, word-finding normalized. No new neurological concerns today. Post-SRS response imaging is scheduled.

3. Resistance mechanism profile (tissue NGS): Complex/polyclonal resistance with high-level MET amplification as primary driver, low-level T790M, and newly acquired PIK3CA E545K / PTEN R130Q as secondary alterations.

4. Systemic therapy: Currently off systemic therapy since osi discontinuation approximately 8 weeks ago (no therapy in interim while awaiting SRS and re-biopsy results). Patient maintains ECOG 1 performance status. Lab values within acceptable range for treatment initiation.

5. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.4) -- likely disease-related or steroid-related post-taper; not transfusion-dependent. Will recheck at next visit.

6. Psychosocial: Patient is anxious but engaged, motivated for continued treatment including clinical trial enrollment.

---

PLAN

1. Second-Line Systemic Therapy -- Bridging Chemotherapy:
Given the complexity of her resistance landscape and the absence of a currently available approved targeted regimen for MET-amplified EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-Tagrisso progression, we will initiate platinum-doublet chemotherapy as bridging systemic therapy. Plan is to proceed with Paraplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus Alimta (500 mg/m2 IV day 1) every 21 days. PD-L1 TPS was only 5% on original biopsy; with the immunosuppressive context of concurrent steroids (recently completed) and low PD-L1, addition of immunotherapy at this time is not favored. Vitamin B12 and folic acid supplementation per standard pem protocol. Dexamethasone pre-medication for Alimta antifolate prophylaxis. Zofran PRN for nausea.

2. Clinical Trial Enrollment -- Primary Goal:
Active inquiry is underway for enrollment on a Phase II investigator-sponsored trial of savolitinib (a highly selective MET inhibitor) in combination with osi rechallenge for patients with MET-amplified, EGFR-mutant NSCLC following progression on third-generation EGFR TKI. The scientific rationale is well-supported: savolitinib would address the MET bypass pathway while Tagrisso rechallenge continues to suppress EGFR-dependent signaling and provides CNS coverage. Patient has been pre-screened and appears to meet eligibility criteria pending confirmatory FISH for MET amplification and final screening laboratories. carbo/Alimta will serve as bridging therapy while trial logistics are finalized; plan is to complete a limited number of chemotherapy cycles (approximately 3-4) before transitioning to the trial if she remains eligible. Trial coordinator has been notified. Informed consent process for the trial will be initiated at next visit.

3. CNS Monitoring:
Post-SRS brain MRI scheduled in approximately 2-4 weeks. Given the favorable CNS penetration profile of osi (to be restarted if clinical trial is initiated), we expect ongoing CNS disease control. Dexamethasone has been successfully tapered. No plans to restart steroids unless neurological symptoms recur.

4. Supportive Care:
- Anti-emetic protocol with ondansetron for chemotherapy days
- Proton pump inhibitor for GI protection during anticipated pem/dexamethasone administration
- Monitor CBC with differential on day 14 of each cycle given anticipated nadir
- If grade 2 or greater neutropenia, will consider adding G-CSF prophylaxis
- Nutritional counseling referral placed given mild weight loss and decreased appetite
- Social work referral offered and patient accepted, for psychosocial support and clinical trial navigation assistance

5. Laboratory / Biomarker Monitoring:
- MET amplification FISH confirmatory result pending; will follow up
- Repeat ctDNA (Guardant360) at cycle 2 to monitor for early molecular response and clonal dynamics on chemotherapy
- CBC, CMP, and LFTs at each cycle visit

6. Return to Clinic:
Patient will return in 3 weeks for cycle 1, day 1 of Paraplatin/Alimta. Lab results will be reviewed at that visit. Trial consent will be initiated.

Extensive time was spent with the patient and her husband today reviewing the NGS results in detail, explaining the concept of acquired resistance and the rationale for the proposed treatment sequence. She expressed understanding and agreement with the plan. She is particularly motivated by the prospect of clinical trial participation. The complexity of her resistance profile was explained in accessible language. All questions were answered.

Cancer is present at this assessment. Patient is systemic-therapy naive at this time (off osi for approximately 8 weeks); CNS disease is under post-SRS local treatment.

At that point she developed systemic progression -- growth of the right upper lobe primary and a new right paratracheal node -- as well as three new brain metastases (left frontal 12 mm, right cerebellar 8 mm, left parietal 6 mm) associated with new-onset bifrontal headaches and transient word-finding difficulties.

At progression, liquid biopsy (Guardant360) identified acquired EGFR T790M at low allele frequency (0.6%), and tissue re-biopsy with repeat FoundationOne CDx revealed a more complex polyclonal resistance landscape: low-level T790M (tissue AF 3.1%), high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.1, FISH-confirmed), and newly acquired PIK3CA p.E545K and PTEN p.R130Q alterations. She underwent Gamma Knife SRS to all three brain lesions without complication, followed by a bridging course of carbo (AUC 5) plus peme (Alimta) for 4 cycles with good tolerability and partial response in the chest (primary tumor decreased from 2.7 cm to 2.1 cm). With high-level MET amplification identified as the likely dominant resistance driver, the molecular tumor board recommended enrollment on this Phase II trial combining savolitinib (a MET inhibitor) with osi rechallenge.

She was started on protocol therapy approximately 8 weeks ago. At week 4 she developed grade 2 bilateral lower extremity pitting edema to the mid-shins, a recognized savolitinib-related toxicity, without evidence of cardiac or hepatic etiology (echocardiogram at that time showed preserved LV function, EF 60-65%, no pericardial effusion). Furosemide 20 mg orally daily was initiated with a good response; edema improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks. She also had return of her acneiform rash at grade 1 with osi rechallenge, which is manageable with topical therapy.

Today she presents for 8-week protocol restaging. Imaging results reviewed (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with and without gadolinium). Key findings:
- Right upper lobe mass decreased from 2.1 cm (at trial entry) to 1.4 cm -- a 33% reduction from trial baseline, and representing a 76% reduction from original diagnostic measurement of 5.8 cm.

Overall assessment: Confirmed partial response on protocol, with excellent CNS response as well. The near-complete resolution of the left parietal lesion and dramatic reduction in the left frontal lesion are particularly encouraging and likely reflect both the ongoing effect of prior SRS and the CNS penetration of Tagrisso.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 65-year-old woman with Stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma with MET amplification as a dominant acquired resistance mechanism, currently on a Phase II clinical trial of savolitinib plus osimertinib. She presents today at 6 months on trial with the following overall impression:

1. DISEASE STATUS -- ONGOING PARTIAL RESPONSE, STABLE:
Imaging today demonstrates a continued and durable partial response. The right upper lobe residual mass is stable at 1.3 cm -- representing roughly a 78% reduction from her diagnostic baseline of 5.8 cm. The intracranial disease continues to improve: the left frontal lesion has nearly resolved (2 mm), the left parietal lesion is no longer visible, and the right cerebellar lesion is stable. There is no evidence of new disease anywhere. This is a very encouraging result at 6 months on trial, and represents her best overall response maintained over time. The molecular correlative data (ctDNA with declining EGFR L858R signal, MET amplification below threshold in plasma) support ongoing biologic suppression of the dominant resistant clone. I discussed these results with Ms. Yuen and her daughter in detail today. She was visibly relieved and expressed gratitude. She understands that this is a partial response and that residual radiologic abnormality at the primary site may represent a mix of fibrotic/treatment-related change and residual viable disease, and that ongoing monitoring is essential.

2. TOLERABILITY / ADVERSE EVENTS:
Overall, she is tolerating the combination well at 6 months. Active grade 1 toxicities include:
   - Grade 1 acneiform rash (face) -- managed with topical clindamycin and moisturizer, acceptable to patient, no dose modification warranted

   - Grade 1 intermittent nausea -- managed PRN with ondansetron as needed
   No grade 2 or higher toxicities are active at this time. No hepatotoxicity, no pneumonitis, no significant QTc prolongation. Renal function is stable. Bone marrow function is adequate. The mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.4) has been stable and is felt to reflect residual treatment-related effect from prior chemotherapy; no intervention planned.
   I reviewed the protocol toxicity grading criteria and adverse event reporting requirements with the patient. She is instructed to call the clinic or on-call service for any new or worsening symptoms, and in particular for any new neurological symptoms, significant dyspnea, chest pain, rash worsening, or fever.

Pelvis: The urinary bladder is well-distended with physiologic FDG activity in the urine. The uterus is normal in size without discrete lesions. No pelvic masses. No pelvic free fluid.

OSSEOUS SURVEY (visualized skeleton, vertex to mid-thigh):
No hypermetabolic osseous foci suspicious for skeletal metastases. No lytic or blastic lesions identified in the visualized axial or appendicular skeleton. Mild multilevel degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, including endplate osteophytosis and mild loss of disc height at L3-L4 and L4-L5, without associated hypermetabolism. No pathological fractures.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal soft tissue masses or hypermetabolic foci in the chest wall or peripheral soft tissues. No subcutaneous nodularity.

IMPRESSION:

1. HYPERMETABOLIC RIGHT UPPER LOBE MASS (5.8 x 4.2 cm, SUVmax 14.3): Large spiculated, pleural-based mass in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe demonstrating intense FDG avidity, consistent with known primary lung adenocarcinoma. The mass abuts and thickens the adjacent parietal pleura; frank chest wall invasion is not definitively demonstrated.

2. IPSILATERAL HILAR ADENOPATHY (N1): Multiple hypermetabolic right hilar lymph nodes, dominant node 1.9 cm, SUVmax 7.8, consistent with nodal metastatic involvement.

3. IPSILATERAL MEDIASTINAL ADENOPATHY (N2): Hypermetabolic right paratracheal (4R) lymph nodes measuring up to 1.4 cm, SUVmax 6.1, consistent with ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement (N2 disease).

4. CONTRALATERAL MEDIASTINAL INVOLVEMENT (N3): Borderline hypermetabolic and enlarged left paratracheal (4L) lymph node, SUVmax 3.4. In the context of the current primary tumor and ipsilateral nodal disease, this finding is highly suspicious for contralateral mediastinal involvement, upgrading nodal status to N3. Pathologic confirmation may be considered.

5. IPSILATERAL SUPRACLAVICULAR ADENOPATHY (N3): Clearly hypermetabolic right supraclavicular lymph node, 1.3 cm, SUVmax 5.2, consistent with supraclavicular nodal involvement (N3 disease by AJCC 8th edition, ipsilateral).

6. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE (M0): No hepatic, adrenal, osseous, or distant soft tissue lesions to suggest systemic hematogenous spread. The brain was not imaged on this examination; dedicated brain MRI with gadolinium is recommended to complete the staging evaluation given the high nodal burden and known adenocarcinoma histology with EGFR mutation.

OVERALL STAGING IMPRESSION: Findings are consistent with at minimum Stage IIIB non-small cell lung carcinoma (T3 N3 M0) based on the combination of primary tumor size with pleural abutment, ipsilateral supraclavicular nodal involvement, and contralateral mediastinal borderline nodal involvement. Brain MRI recommended to exclude intracranial metastases prior to definitive treatment planning.

Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 65-year-old woman with Stage IV (M1b, brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, right upper lobe, presenting today for her protocol-specified 6-month on-trial assessment visit.
