HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Yuen's oncologic history is well-known. Briefly, she was diagnosed approximately two years ago with Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, confirmed on CT-guided core biopsy. Initial FoundationOne CDx demonstrated EGFR L858R (exon 21, allele frequency 38.2%), co-mutant TP53 R248W, and low-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4) at baseline. PD-L1 TPS was 5% by 22C3. She was treated with Tagrisso 80 mg daily as first-line therapy (not a surgical candidate given N3 nodal disease at diagnosis), achieving an excellent initial partial response -- the primary mass shrank from 5.8 cm to approximately 1.8 cm by 12 months, with near-complete nodal response.

She developed disease progression at approximately 14 months on osi, manifesting as worsening headaches and mild word-finding difficulties. MRI at that time demonstrated three new intracranial metastases (left frontal 12 mm, right cerebellum 8 mm, left parietal 6 mm), and concurrent CT chest showed growth of the right upper lobe mass to 2.7 cm with a new right paratracheal lymph node. Liquid biopsy (Guardant360) identified an acquired EGFR T790M (allele frequency 0.6%, atypically low for Tagrisso failure) alongside EGFR L858R. Tissue re-biopsy with FoundationOne CDx re-profiling revealed a complex, heterogeneous resistance landscape including: low-level T790M (3.1%), newly acquired PIK3CA E545K and PTEN R130Q, and critically, high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.1). The molecular tumor board identified MET amplification as the likely dominant driver of resistance.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) to all three brain metastases approximately 6 weeks after progression was identified, with excellent neurological recovery. Following SRS, she received four cycles of bridging carbo (AUC 5) plus peme every 21 days, achieving a partial response in the chest and stable/responding CNS disease. She was then enrolled on the current Phase II trial of savolitinib plus Tagrisso, which she initiated approximately 6 months ago.

Since enrolling on trial, she has had one protocol-specified restaging at 8 weeks, which showed continued partial response -- the right upper lobe residual mass decreased from 2.1 cm (at trial entry) to 1.4 cm, with further shrinkage or resolution of the intracranial lesions. MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
63-year-old East Asian female with newly diagnosed right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma histology, Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) per recent PET-CT. EGFR L858R mutation detected on tissue biopsy. Referred for brain MRI for completeness of initial staging workup and to evaluate for occult intracranial metastatic disease prior to initiation of systemic therapy. No focal neurological deficits reported at time of imaging referral.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3 Tesla scanner. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1, axial T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR (fluid-attenuated inversion recovery), axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T2* gradient echo, and axial T1 fat-saturated post-gadolinium sequences (including thin-section post-contrast volumetric MPRAGE through the entire brain). Gadolinium-based contrast agent (Multihance, gadobenate dimeglumine, 0.1 mmol/kg IV) was administered without complication. Delayed post-contrast images were obtained approximately 10 minutes following contrast injection to optimize detection of small leptomeningeal or parenchymal lesions. Total imaging time approximately 45 minutes. No significant motion artifact. Images are of diagnostic quality.

FINDINGS:

Parenchyma:
The cerebral hemispheres are symmetric. There is no discrete parenchymal mass, nodule, or focal area of abnormal signal that would suggest an intraparenchymal metastatic deposit. No rim-enhancing or solid-enhancing lesions are identified within the supratentorial or infratentorial compartments. No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia or hypercellular tumor foci. No abnormal susceptibility signal on SWI to suggest hemorrhagic metastases or microhemorrhages beyond scattered chronic changes (see below). The gray-white matter junction appears intact throughout, with no nodular enhancement along the cortical ribbon to suggest small cortical metastases.

White Matter:
There are a few small, scattered T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensities in the periventricular and deep subcortical white matter bilaterally, predominantly along the frontal and parietal white matter. These are non-enhancing and do not demonstrate restricted diffusion. Their distribution and morphology are most consistent with chronic microvascular (small vessel ischemic) changes, appropriate for the patient's age and likely related to her cardiovascular risk profile. No confluent white matter signal abnormality. No demyelinating lesion morphology.

Ventricles and Cerebrospinal Fluid Spaces:
The lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle are all normal in caliber and configuration. There is no hydrocephalus. The cerebral sulci and basal cisterns are patent and of appropriate caliber for a 63-year-old. There is no evidence of sulcal crowding or obliteration to suggest raised intracranial pressure. The sylvian fissures are symmetric.

Leptomeninges and Dura:
No abnormal leptomeningeal enhancement is identified on the post-gadolinium sequences, including the delayed acquisitions. No dural-based masses or pachymeningeal thickening is seen. The dural venous sinuses (superior sagittal sinus, transverse sinuses, sigmoid sinuses) appear patent and of normal caliber on the SWI and post-contrast sequences. No abnormal meningeal enhancement along the cranial nerves or posterior fossa.

Posterior Fossa:
The cerebellum is symmetric and without focal lesions. No mass, nodule, or abnormal enhancement identified in either cerebellar hemisphere, the vermis, or the cerebellar tonsils. The cerebellar tonsils are in normal position, above the level of the foramen magnum. The brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla) is normal in morphology and signal intensity. The cervicomedullary junction is unremarkable.

Sellar and Parasellar Regions:
The pituitary gland is of normal size and configuration. No pituitary mass identified. The optic chiasm and optic nerves are intact. The cavernous sinuses are symmetric without filling defects.

Skull and Calvarium:
The osseous calvarium is intact. No lytic or sclerotic calvarial lesions suggestive of osseous metastatic involvement. The outer and inner tables are preserved throughout. The mastoid air cells and middle ear cavities are clear.

Orbits:
The globes and orbits are symmetrically normal. No retrobulbar mass or proptosis. The extraocular muscles appear unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. No intracranial metastatic disease identified. Specifically, no parenchymal enhancing lesions, no leptomeningeal enhancement, and no mass effect or midline shift are present. This is reassuring in the setting of newly diagnosed locally advanced non-small cell lung carcinoma.

2. Small, scattered periventricular and deep white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities bilaterally, non-enhancing, most consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic change appropriate for age. No acute ischemic or demyelinating etiology identified.

3. Ventricles and sulci normal in caliber for age. No hydrocephalus.

4. Intact calvarium without osseous destructive lesions.

Interpretation:
EGFR p.L858R is a well-characterized activating point mutation in exon 21 of the epidermal growth factor receptor gene, resulting in constitutive activation of downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling. This mutation is detected in approximately 40–45% of EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancers in East Asian populations and approximately 25–30% in Western populations. The allele frequency of 38.2% is consistent with the estimated tumor purity and is consistent with a clonal, founding mutation. EGFR exon 21 L858R is an established predictive biomarker for response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) including erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib, dacomitinib, and osimertinib. Per current guidelines (NCCN, ESMO, IASLC), patients with EGFR-activating mutations should be offered first-line EGFR TKI therapy; osimertinib (Tagrisso) demonstrates superior progression-free and overall survival compared with first- and second-generation TKIs in this mutation context (FLAURA trial). This mutation is a companion diagnostic (CDx) biomarker per FDA-approved labeling.

TP53 p.R248W:
TP53 p.R248W is one of the most frequently observed gain-of-function/dominant-negative TP53 missense mutations in human cancer, occurring at the critical DNA-contact residue arginine 248 within the DNA-binding domain. This mutation abolishes wild-type tumor suppressor function and confers oncogenic gain-of-function properties, promoting genomic instability, resistance to apoptosis, and dysregulated cell cycle progression. In EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, co-occurring TP53 mutations are detected in approximately 40–50% of cases and have been associated with somewhat shorter progression-free survival on first-generation EGFR TKIs in retrospective analyses, though data for osimertinib in this context are limited. The allele frequency of 42.1% — slightly higher than the EGFR L858R allele frequency — is consistent with TP53 mutation occurring early in tumor evolution (possibly in a pre-malignant clone) or in a slightly more prevalent subclone. There are no approved therapies directly targeting TP53 p.R248W at this time, though several investigational approaches (including APR-246/eprenetapopt) are in clinical development for TP53-mutant malignancies. This co-mutation should be noted for its potential prognostic relevance and as a baseline comparator for future molecular profiling at disease progression.

SMARCA4 p.R1192H:
SMARCA4 encodes BRG1, the catalytic ATPase subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex. Pathogenic loss-of-function mutations in SMARCA4 define a distinct subset of NSCLC (SMARCA4-deficient thoracic sarcomatoid carcinoma), but this variant at p.R1192H has not been functionally characterized and is reported as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) in available databases (ClinVar, COSMIC). The allele frequency of 14.8% suggests this may represent a subclonal alteration. Its functional impact on SWI/SNF activity or EGFR TKI sensitivity is unknown. No approved targeted therapies apply.

CDH1 p.E243K:
CDH1 encodes E-cadherin, a transmembrane glycoprotein central to epithelial cell–cell adhesion. Pathogenic CDH1 mutations are associated with hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC) and lobular breast cancer, though somatic CDH1 alterations are observed in lung adenocarcinoma at low frequency. The p.E243K variant has not been definitively classified as pathogenic in lung cancer and is reported as a VUS. The allele frequency of 9.3% suggests a subclonal occurrence. No clinical actionability in the lung cancer context at this time. Given the patient's personal and family cancer history, germline implications of CDH1 p.E243K should be considered in the context of genetic counseling if not already performed, though somatic-only status must be evaluated.

Interpretation: This tumor does not meet biomarker criteria for pembrolizumab (Keytruda) based on TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb) or MSI-H. TMB of 4 mut/Mb is in the low range for NSCLC. Additionally, in the clinical context of an EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, the benefit of PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor therapy is substantially attenuated compared with EGFR wild-type NSCLC. Multiple clinical trials (IMpower150 subset analyses, KEYNOTE-789) have demonstrated limited single-agent immunotherapy activity in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, and concurrent EGFR TKI plus checkpoint inhibitor combinations are associated with excess pneumonitis toxicity. FoundationOne CDx performs tumor-only sequencing on FFPE tissue and does not include a paired germline control. Variants detected by tumor-only profiling may represent somatic tumor mutations, germline variants, or clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). The following observations are noted:

- CDH1 p.E243K (VUS, AF 9.3%): CDH1 germline pathogenic variants are associated with hereditary diffuse gastric cancer syndrome and lobular breast cancer predisposition. Although p.E243K is currently classified as a VUS and the allele frequency of 9.3% does not by itself suggest a germline origin (germline variants are typically present at ~50% AF in tumor-only sequencing if heterozygous), the finding in the context of a family history of cancer warrants consideration of germline genetic counseling referral to formally evaluate hereditary cancer risk, particularly if the patient has not previously undergone germline testing.

- TP53 p.R248W (AF 42.1%): Allele frequency compatible with either somatic clonal mutation or possible germline (Li-Fraumeni syndrome) context. Given patient age at lung cancer diagnosis (63 years) and no reported history of early-onset malignancies, a germline TP53 origin is considered low probability but cannot be excluded without paired germline sequencing. Referral for genetic counseling is reasonable if clinically indicated.

This comprehensive genomic profile of a right upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma in a 63-year-old East Asian female with a remote smoking history reveals:

1. EGFR p.L858R (exon 21) — Clonal, activating, pathogenic driver mutation at allele frequency 38.2%. This is the primary oncogenic driver and a companion diagnostic biomarker for EGFR TKI therapy (erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib, dacomitinib, osimertinib). Osimertinib (Tagrisso, third-generation, CNS-penetrant EGFR TKI) is the preferred first-line agent per NCCN guidelines and FLAURA trial data.

2. TP53 p.R248W (exon 7) — Co-occurring pathogenic TP53 gain-of-function mutation at high allele frequency (42.1%), likely an early clonal event. No direct therapeutic actionability; prognostic relevance as a co-mutation in EGFR-mutant NSCLC should be noted.

4. CDH1 p.E243K (exon 6) — VUS, subclonal (AF 9.3%). Germline implications uncertain; genetic counseling referral may be considered.

5. MET low-level copy number gain (ratio 2.4) — Below threshold for actionable MET amplification. Serial monitoring at progression recommended given MET amplification as a known EGFR TKI acquired resistance mechanism.

6. CCND1 gain — Low-level, no direct actionability.

7. TMB Low (4 mut/Mb), MSS — Does not support checkpoint inhibitor use based on these biomarkers.

8. No EGFR T790M, no EGFR exon 19 deletion, no KRAS mutation, no ALK/ROS1/RET/NTRK fusion, no HER2 amplification/mutation, no MET exon 14 skip, no STK11/KEAP1/NF1 mutations detected.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FoundationOne CDx (F1CDx) is a next-generation sequencing-based in vitro diagnostic device (IVD) approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (PMA P170019). The assay profiles 324 cancer-related genes for substitutions, insertions/deletions, copy number alterations, and select gene rearrangements/fusions. The assay is performed on FFPE tissue using hybrid capture-based enrichment followed by massively parallel paired-end sequencing on an Illumina HiSeq platform. Minimum tumor content: 20% (pathologist-estimated). Minimum DNA input: 50 ng. TMB is calculated from somatic synonymous and non-synonymous mutations across the sequenced region (approximately 1.1 Mb). MSI is assessed across 95 intronic repeat loci. Sequencing depth: >500x mean coverage across targeted regions. Sensitivity for substitutions and small indels: >99% at tumor fraction ≥20%. This assay is a companion diagnostic for multiple FDA-approved therapies including osimertinib (Tagrisso) for EGFR exon 19 deletions and exon 21 L858R substitutions, erlotinib (Tarceva), gefitinib (Iressa), afatinib (Gilotrif), dacomitinib (Vizimpro), and pembrolizumab (Keytruda) for TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Yuen is a 63-year-old East Asian woman, former smoker, referred for evaluation and management of a newly diagnosed right upper lobe lung mass with regional lymphadenopathy, biopsy-confirmed non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma histology. She presents today with approximately 3 months of progressive cough, two episodes of mild hemoptysis, and an 8-pound unintentional weight loss.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Yuen first noticed a persistent, non-productive cough approximately 3 months ago, which she initially attributed to a post-nasal drip in the setting of seasonal allergies. Over the ensuing weeks, the cough became progressively more frequent and productive of small amounts of clear to white sputum. Roughly 6 weeks into her symptoms, she experienced two discrete episodes of blood-streaked sputum -- she describes coughing up approximately a teaspoon of bright red blood on two separate occasions, separated by about one week. These episodes alarmed her and prompted her to seek evaluation by her primary care physician. She additionally reports exertional dyspnea over the past 4 to 6 weeks -- she becomes short of breath climbing one flight of stairs, whereas previously she had no limitations with ambulation. She denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or lower extremity edema. There is no pleuritic chest pain and no fever or chills. She endorses an 8-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months. She has noticed a mild decrease in appetite but denies early satiety.

Her primary care physician obtained a chest X-ray, which demonstrated a right upper lobe opacity. This was followed by a CT chest with contrast, which revealed a right upper lobe mass measuring approximately 5.8 cm with chest wall abutment and regional lymphadenopathy. She was then referred to interventional radiology for CT-guided transthoracic core needle biopsy of the right upper lobe mass. Three cores were obtained. Pathology returned invasive adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns. IHC confirmed TTF-1 positivity and Napsin-A positivity. EGFR PCR (cobas) performed on the biopsy specimen identified an EGFR L858R mutation (exon 21). ALK IHC (D5F3) was negative. ROS1 IHC was negative. PD-L1 by 22C3 was 5% (TPS). For staging purposes, a whole-body PET-CT was obtained, demonstrating the hypermetabolic right upper lobe primary (SUVmax 14.3), hypermetabolic right hilar and right paratracheal lymph nodes, a mildly hypermetabolic left paratracheal (4L) node, and a hypermetabolic right supraclavicular lymph node (SUVmax 5.2, 1.3 cm), establishing N3 disease. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases were identified. An MRI brain with and without gadolinium was also obtained and showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease -- only scattered periventricular white matter T2/FLAIR changes consistent with chronic small vessel disease. Comprehensive genomic profiling via FoundationOne CDx was sent from the biopsy specimen; results are reviewed in detail below. This case was reviewed at multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor board in advance of today's consultation.

She presents today for discussion of her diagnosis, staging, and treatment planning.

- ALK (D5F3 IHC): Negative

- Microsatellite status: MSS
- Actionable alteration: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, pathogenic, AF 38.2%)
- Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (exon 7, pathogenic, AF 42.1%); SMARCA4 p.R1192H (VUS); CDH1 p.E243K (VUS)
- Copy number: MET amplification low-level (CNR 2.4, not high-level); CCND1 gain

ASSESSMENT
1. Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) non-small cell lung cancer, right upper lobe, adenocarcinoma histology, EGFR L858R-mutant.
   - Tumor is 5.8 cm with parietal pleural invasion (T3), N3 by virtue of ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node involvement confirmed on PET-CT and likely contralateral mediastinal (4L) nodal involvement. M0 by PET-CT and MRI brain.
   - The presence of an EGFR L858R-activating mutation is the dominant and actionable driver. The TP53 co-mutation is present at high allele frequency and is a common co-mutation in EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma; this does not change management at this time but may have prognostic relevance. The low-level MET copy number gain is not high-level and is not considered actionable currently.
   - PD-L1 at 5% is low; immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors is not appropriate in EGFR-mutant NSCLC and contraindicated as monotherapy in this setting.
2. ECOG performance status 1. Patient is fully ambulatory and employed as a teacher.
3. Mild anemia of chronic disease.
4. Comorbidities: Well-controlled hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteopenia -- none of which preclude systemic therapy.

PLAN
This case was reviewed at our multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor board in advance of today's visit. The case was discussed with thoracic surgery and radiation oncology colleagues.

Surgical resectability: Due to involvement of the right supraclavicular lymph node (N3 disease), which by AJCC 8th edition staging criteria constitutes Stage IIIB disease, Ms. Yuen is not a candidate for surgical resection. This was reviewed and agreed upon at tumor board.

Radiation oncology consultation: Radiation oncology was consulted as part of the tumor board review. In an EGFR-mutant Stage III unresectable NSCLC, the role of concurrent chemoradiation followed by Imfinzi (as in the PACIFIC paradigm) is more nuanced, given that Imfinzi has not demonstrated benefit and may be harmful in EGFR-mutant tumors in this context. Furthermore, the LAURA trial has demonstrated meaningful progression-free survival benefit for Tagrisso following concurrent chemoradiation specifically in EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC. However, Ms. Yuen has expressed strong concerns about the toxicity profile of concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation, including risks of esophagitis, pneumonitis, and myelosuppression. After thorough discussion of the risk-benefit profile and review of available evidence at tumor board, the following approach has been agreed upon.

Systemic therapy recommendation: Given the presence of an EGFR-activating mutation (L858R), the LAURA trial data supporting Tagrisso (Tagrisso) in unresectable Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC, and the patient's preference to avoid concurrent chemoradiation toxicity, the recommendation is to proceed with osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as definitive/curative-intent therapy. The rationale for selecting osi over earlier-generation EGFR TKIs (Tarceva, Iressa, Gilotrif) includes its superior CNS penetration (relevant given the high propensity for brain metastases in EGFR-mutant NSCLC), its more favorable toxicity profile, and its demonstrated superiority in EGFR-mutant NSCLC across settings. osi will be initiated as first-line systemic therapy.

I have had an extensive discussion with Ms. Yuen and her husband regarding her diagnosis, its implications, the rationale for the proposed treatment, and realistic expectations. We discussed:
- The nature of Stage IIIB disease and what it means for treatment intent (curative-intent in this setting)
- The mechanism of action of osi as a third-generation, irreversible EGFR TKI
- Expected common adverse effects: acneiform rash (face, trunk, and scalp), paronychia, diarrhea, dry skin, stomatitis, and, rarely, interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis (approximately 3-4% incidence) and QT prolongation
- The need for close monitoring with repeat imaging at approximately 3-month intervals
- The importance of sun protection given potential rash exacerbation with UV exposure
- The importance of reporting new neurological symptoms promptly given the risk of CNS disease
- Drug interactions: She should avoid concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin, St. John's Wort); her current medications do not pose significant CYP interaction concerns

Ms. Yuen and her husband asked several informed questions and demonstrated a clear understanding of the plan. She is agreeable to proceeding with Tagrisso and expresses a strong preference for oral outpatient therapy over intravenous chemotherapy or radiation. She is motivated and engaged in her care.

Additional management:
- Baseline EKG to be obtained prior to Tagrisso initiation (QTc assessment)
- Baseline ophthalmologic evaluation recommended given rare EGFR TKI-associated ocular toxicity (keratitis/dry eye)
- Continue amlodipine, atorvastatin, calcium, and vitamin D as current
- Bone health: Recommend DEXA scan update given systemic therapy initiation and known osteopenia; consider bisphosphonate therapy if osteoporosis confirmed
- Genetic counseling referral offered given family history of lung cancer in paternal uncle; patient interested, referral placed
- Nutritional consultation referral placed given 8-pound weight loss and decreased appetite
- Social work referral placed to assess psychosocial needs and connect patient with lung cancer support resources
- Smoking cessation: Not applicable -- patient has been abstinent from tobacco for 18 years
- Return to clinic in 3 to 4 weeks to assess early tolerance of Tagrisso; repeat imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast) planned at 3 months
- Patient provided with written information about osi, contact information for our triage nurse line, and instructed to call immediately for new headache, neurological symptoms, progressive dyspnea, or skin reactions that are severe or affecting daily activities

Cancer is present at this assessment.

RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known Stage IIIB right upper lobe non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma histology, EGFR L858R-mutant. On osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily for approximately 3 months. Restaging examination to assess treatment response.

COMPARISON: PET-CT performed at initial staging (approximately 3 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 90 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast material using a portal venous phase acquisition protocol. Axial images were obtained at 2.5 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reformats in the coronal and sagittal planes. Lung windows were reviewed at -1500/+500 HU; mediastinal windows at -400/+40 HU. No oral contrast was administered. Images acquired on a 64-slice MDCT scanner.

--- FINDINGS ---

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The right upper lobe mass, previously measuring 5.8 x 4.2 cm on the initial staging PET-CT, has markedly diminished in size. On the current study, this lesion measures approximately 3.1 x 2.4 cm in its greatest axial dimensions on mediastinal windows. The mass remains centered in the right upper lobe along the posterior and apical segments, abutting the major fissure medially. Previously noted contact with the chest wall / parietal pleura is substantially reduced; the lesion now appears to maintain only an indirect relationship to the lateral chest wall without definitive pleural tethering on this non-contrast-enhanced lung window review. The margins of the mass appear partially spiculated with some internal architectural distortion. There is no cavitation. No satellite nodules are identified in the right upper lobe or elsewhere in the lungs.

Bilaterally, the lungs are otherwise clear. No new focal consolidation, ground-glass opacification, or pleural-based nodules. No endobronchial lesion. Central airways are patent. The visualized bronchial tree demonstrates no endoluminal abnormality. No new pulmonary nodules are identified.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The right hilar lymphadenopathy has substantially regressed. Previously enlarged right hilar nodes measuring up to 1.9 cm in short axis are now markedly reduced, with the dominant right hilar node measuring approximately 0.9 cm in short axis — within the upper limits of normal for this station and representing a significant reduction from prior. No discrete right hilar mass effect on the right main or intermediate bronchi.

Previously hypermetabolic right paratracheal lymph nodes (station 4R) have decreased in size; the dominant 4R node previously measuring approximately 1.4 cm in short axis is now approximately 0.7 cm, well below pathologic threshold. No pathologic lymphadenopathy is identified in the subcarinal (station 7), aortopulmonary window (station 5/6), or left paratracheal (station 4L) regions.

Right supraclavicular adenopathy previously identified on PET-CT as a 1.3 cm hypermetabolic node is no longer discretely visualized as a measurable enlarged node on the current CT examination; no right supraclavicular lymph node meets pathologic size criteria at this time.

Pleura and Pericardium:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No evidence of pleural thickening or nodularity to suggest pleural involvement. Pericardium is unremarkable. No pericardial effusion.

No chest wall invasion identified. Subcutaneous tissues are unremarkable. Mild bilateral gynecomastia not clinically significant.

Heart is normal in size. Coronary artery calcifications are absent. Ascending and descending thoracic aorta are normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation or dissection. Thoracic aortic contour is smooth. Main pulmonary artery measures 2.6 cm, within normal limits. Superior vena cava and venous structures are patent. No vascular encasement by the residual right upper lobe mass.

ABDOMEN:

The liver is normal in size with a smooth contour. Hepatic parenchyma enhances uniformly. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. Portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size, shape, and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi identified. No perirenal abnormality. A small 4 mm hypodense lesion in the right kidney is too small to characterize but is statistically most likely a simple cyst. Ureters are not dilated.

Retroperitoneum and Abdominal Lymph Nodes:

PELVIS:

Uterus appears mildly atrophic, consistent with postmenopausal status. No adnexal mass or free pelvic fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Ovaries are not separately identified, consistent with postmenopausal involution.

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked reduction in the size of the right upper lobe primary lung mass, now measuring approximately 3.1 x 2.4 cm compared to 5.8 x 4.2 cm on the prior staging PET-CT obtained approximately 3 months ago. This represents a decrease of roughly 47% in the longest axial dimension, consistent with a substantial radiographic response to osimertinib therapy.

2. Significant regression of right hilar adenopathy, with the dominant right hilar node now measuring 0.9 cm (previously up to 1.9 cm). Right paratracheal (4R) nodal burden similarly markedly decreased, now measuring 0.7 cm (previously approximately 1.4 cm short axis). 4. No evidence of metastatic disease in the abdomen or pelvis. No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No pathologic abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

5. No osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement throughout the imaged skeleton.

Overall, findings are consistent with a meaningful and favorable response to EGFR-directed therapy. The significant decrease in both the primary tumor and regional nodal disease supports ongoing treatment. Correlation with clinical status and laboratory parameters is recommended.
